confidence_detail: Topics derived from TF-IDF vectorization and K-means clustering over Whisper-transcribed oral history audio. Cluster labels editorially assigned 2026-05-25 based on top terms and representative chunk review. Transcription errors from Whisper (base model) propagate into topic assignments. Topics are research aids and entry points, not definitive content analysis.

No two ways about it, but they felt very close to Christ and they would sing all the time. They'd sing gospels all the time. Well, we were going to the White House. Just to remember, they were flashing back thinking, God, the bus that we had then, we had three good tires and one that we wrapped with rags, the whole together or something like that. It was just like, I think it added years on to their life because they almost all have the same health kind of problems, prostate cancer and certainly bad hearts because of their diet. You know, everything was deep fried. They were told that was good for you except for a central page. He said, you know, you never should eat this kind of stuff because it'll kill you. Kill your gun. I don't know how he learned that or who told him it. He was right. He was one of the few ball players that took care of himself physically in the kind of food he ate. What didn't mean that he was not trying to build this kind of a saint because he wasn't. He had a mean streak like everybody else and just like our president was probably a big womanizer as, you know, as a gentleman is and probably always will be. So where we go from here? I would ask you what impact has this had on your life with the new game players? Well, it's kept me in touch with what it is like never to be accepted. You see, I'm white. Being white, I can go places that other humans can't. I know that now. I've forgotten what I was like. I remembered back in the late 60s, if you ever saw pictures of me, I had a beard that was down to here. My hair was screaming all. I was considered to be being an artist at that time since you were very poor, unacceptable as a human. I know what it's like to go into places where you're beat up or things are thrown at you. You try, you know, my car was bashed up a number of times by Rednecks and whites who would see your painted car or painted auto and they think, I want to destroy this bastard. I don't care. You know, and being a small person, you're an easy hit. But I'm still white. So from that standpoint, I could get my hair cut. I could get a shave. I could drive, use ordinary transportation and I could melt right back into society. These guys never could. And it's one of the things that I've had to remember time and time again that no matter how unfortunate I am now, I mean, I'm fortunate now, but on where's

topics.label: The Negro Leagues Legacy
representative_chunks: first and foremost, he was the finest human being I've ever met. You know, Buck was just an amazing human being who I got to spend a great deal of time with. He was the volunteer chairman of the board here for the Negro League baseball museum and really is the person who is the architect in having built this great museum. And he gave the last 16 years of his life to build a museum that hopefully will stand a test of time to pay tribute to those men and women who I like to refer to as America's unsung baseball heroes so that they will never be forgotten. Obviously, Buck was a great player himself in the Negro League, predominantly with the Kansas City Monarchs, where he not only played first base here, but eventually became a player manager. And then moved to the major leagues as a groundbreaking scout where he signed Hall of Famers, Lou Brock and Ernie Banks to their first professional contract. Hopefully future Hall of Famers and Leigh Arthur Smith and Joe Carter to their first professional contract. And then of course, broke barriers himself becoming the first African American coach in major league baseball history in 1962. And so Buck's career was very significant, but he's best known as name is his grand ambassador for our sport. And of course, his role where he was narrating during Ken Burns's epic documentary on the history of baseball really catapulted him into national international stardom. He was 82 when that documentary came out and he passed away at age 94, months, sharpest, 95th birthday, but he really became the voice of the Negro League. He's the person that really helped a lot of people around the world care about the Negro League. And he brought those leagues to life with his wonderful stories and the charisma that he displayed particularly in the Burns documentary. And people fell in love with this story, but you know, you think about it. What's not to love about the underdog overcoming adversity to go on to greatness? That's the American story. Talking here with Bob Kendrick, president of the Negro League baseball museum. Now Bob, obviously we know the Negro leagues was enriched with talent as you mentioned on multiple occasions here in this interview. But you know, is there any players, there any one player or there any few players, several players that you know that would have definitely made fantastic major leagues because you know, obviously most most of the great ones weren't necessarily granted an opportunity. Oh man, I tell you, we already have enough time to show to go through all of them, but you know, cool pop of bill who is still believed to be the fastest man to ever play this game. Never hit less than 300 against all levels of competition, Negro League, including exhibition games against major leagues. You know, he stole bases at the lightning speed, great defensive outbuilder

named route that once. Well, I don't know about an independent traveling team, but they were frequently all star teams that barns stormed and tried to get a game where they could. They would play, for example, in Philadelphia, and their manager would be on the phone while they were playing trying to get a game the next day with some team. But the Negro League's themselves were, in fact, a defined league. They had specific teams. They had all star games which were played. And all of that came to an end in 1950. Many people confuse Negro League's with black ball teams. There is a distinction. The Negro League's were a defined league with its rules and regulations and so forth to my knowledge. Whereas after they ended, there were still black ball teams playing one another, but that was not the Negro League. Okay. Well, how many teams did both go ahead? Baltimore had two well-known teams, the most recent being the Baltimore League giants. And prior to that, there was the Baltimore Black socks. Who was the owners of the Black Sacks and the League Giants, if you know? Okay. Can you tell me about this post on the wall here? And generally, centered around some sports memorabilia or baseball cards show most recently they've had such a meeting in Chantilly, Virginia. They get together as many of the people as are healthy and well enough to come. And that is becoming increasingly more difficult. They also have the Major League's has an Assistance Program where they will assist some of these players that may be having difficulties of one kind or another. But in 1990, as you can see, some former Major Liggers were there, Monti Irvin and Joe Black. And they had some Leon Day who was deceased. Yes. Who is deceased but in the Hall of Fame. Double duty Radcliffe, Buck Leonard, Max Manning, Cool Pop of Bell, Lester Lockert, just a Gene Bonson, Stan Lake Lahn, Wilmer Fields, who Wilmer is the current president of the Negro League baseball players association. And just a very genuine good group of men. And meeting these men that you ever discussed, have you ever witnessed a Negro-based football league, gained yourself or? No. I'm old but I'm not that old. Okay. Now, you mentioned some of the guys that you met and what was your impression of some of these gentlemen? Well, they were, I think that you have said it when you used the word gentlemen. To a man, they are gentlemen. They are well-spoken people. They are honest. They have many fond and good memories of baseball. They played because they loved the game and because they enjoyed the game, because they enjoyed playing with one another. And they

their uniforms, their gloves, their balls, their bats, and so forth. Someone has it. Whether it's family members, some of it of course may be in Cooperstown, some of it may be in the Kansas City Museum, or one or more other types of places of that sort. But there is still equipment around in people that have it that, as I said, would in one way or another make it available to the Baltimore Museum. Additionally, there are photographs. There is correspondence. There are all sorts of schedules and printed paper types of things, newspapers and of that nature. There are maybe stock certificates from some of these teams. There may be motion pictures. Particularly, there is a film which is known to exist known as the Quincy Troop film, which is an interview in, I don't know when it was, 1990 or 1991, of many of the players as to their experiences and what they knew. There's things of this nature that are around. Do you think a exhibit of this nature would help baseball in Baltimore or hinder it? I think it would most definitely help baseball in Baltimore. I think it would enormously help the Bay of Ruth Museum, and I think it would have great attendance and great interest. Okay. As a Baltimoreian myself, I was a little not even about the neighborhood baseball league. Do you think a lot of Baltimoreians are as just as not even as I? Yes. And I think many major leagueers are just as naive as you, and many baseball people are just as naive of you. The point that I made was that the Negro leagues and black ball players are not the same thing. And it is that about which there has been some confusion. There is one and only one official Negro league. There is one and only one official Negro league baseball players association of Negro league players, and this is it. There may be pretenders, but this is it. This is the real thing. And then the original, do you have a date from when it began and when it ended? When it began, I don't recall, but as I said, it came to an end in 1950. After 1951, there was no more Negro league baseball. And I think it is very helpful, particularly to the predominantly schools that have a predominantly black enrollment. I think that students relate to these players. Saturdays he shines shoes and on Sunday he used to umpire all the ball games so he knew most a lot of people some people still work in the city hall my father used to umpire ball games when they play so I think my experience was a little bit different however I do remember seeing some things that were more so after the war than before the war but I imagine when we first moved to Richmond the and everybody was poor white people in black people so I thought that people respected one another more when they were in the same condition I remember we used to all go over to people until this lady's house and there'd be white people in black people and they'd have French bread and salami and they have Italian wine and that you know everybody sort of umpits didn't yeah but the black people still had their own churches and the white people still went to there well most of the Italian people were Catholic I guess and we had that was most the var social activity in north Richmond was going to church and people would go fishing and I remember when I was a child we used to play in the Indian Mounds of course I didn't know that they were Indian Mounds and dig up all the shells and the hair and the bones and all the other artifacts I guess we just threw them away and we used to go down um what street is that um not Willard but it's the first street as you come in north Richmond and we would go over where uh standard oil has all the settling ponds at that time the bay used to come in to north Richmond there was an inlet and we used

don't know where they they didn't turn them but I think they just made a move out of the area I think because in North Richmond the what used to be a re-manufacturing company I think they did bathtubs or something but during war time they converted into war time manufacturing I guess casings for shells and this sort of thing and our bullets whatever and they had the shipyards in Richmond so they took them away too and recently I went to reunion for North Richmond was about two years ago and her father was talking about how they moved them out of North Richmond because of the war time activities and I also remember that there used to be an orphanage I guess and in the El Srito Hills and I think it's where they now have a school I don't know the streets it's a big building they have a college there yeah that used to be an orphanage for Chinese boys because they would all I remember they all come into Richmond and they would all have on blue sweaters I think in yellow hats and that's most of the time that we saw a lot of Chinese people in Richmond most of the time as I said we used to walk a lot so we would walk to the plunge and I understand at one time but not when I was a child I don't remember that they didn't allow black people in the plunge but I've heard other people say that they did not and I also remember going up into El Srito Brandy and that was just Hills San Pablo was nothing we would go out to what is now third I guess it's called Run-Roll Road in market and there was only one two buildings and we would call that sunny side there's a feet feet storm we would call that sunny side there were no other buildings around that was just setting out there and my father said that when he was a child his father used to walk from Berkeley overtale subriding and they'd go hunting for rabbits and he remember one time his father asked him he used to beat the bushes and then the rabbits would run out and his father would shoot him and he went and hit this particular bush in a wild cat a bobcat ran out and he said there were a lot of bobcats in that area my father did oral history for the University of California so you may be able to get something from them about when he used to play ball I remember we used to come down to first street park and they'd have ball games and Richmond do you remember when that was there there was a big ball park at first at least it was big to me I thought it was large and the only kind of activities well I remember every Saturday we used to

became you know people we would see all the time we didn't see anymore you know it was like you lived some like far away and you'd go downtown Richmond and I remember there was this Reverend Davis and his daughters went to school with me they came from I guess it's not Redlands but down someplace in Southern California and near Indio California and he used to stand on he was a minister and he used to stand on the corner and ask for money so when we get out of school we'd go and help collect money for Reverend Davis he was going to build a church and that church is still in North Richmond at the corner of he did built the church from money he collected from the shipyard days it's on the corner of Chesley and Truman Street I think so and let's see what else do I remember the we in North Richmond we only had one little building and Mr. Hill had a boy scout group and sometimes we go down near and have activities but most of the things we did were very limited the McGrues and they still live here they live in the layout I think that Mr. McGru helped my father find a house when he first came to North Richmond a lot of the Spanish families and some Indian families used to live down on the Santa Fe land which is now St. John's and they had housing that was free to them because they worked at the Santa Fe yards are either Santa Fe provided them that property because at one time they took land from the Indians or something I never was I never found out the correct thing about that one of the bad things I remember about living in North Richmond and this became worse as more people got automobiles but is that a lot of people used to get killed in Richmond with the railroad tracks you know people would get killed crossing for like 47th Street because there were no barriers crossing and in North Richmond a lot of people I remember one of our neighbors husbands got killed at the one on Chesley Avenue so it was always dangerous to cross the railroad tracks and then I remember when I was a child I was telling my children the other day that my father worked at Pullman Company and he never made a lot of money so he had all these other jobs but every Friday I guess Friday evening my mother used to go shopping and we're downtown Richmond on the near the corner of Harborway and McDonald's there used to be a safeway store and so she used to go shopping in there and we'd buy a whole box of groceries for five dollars and then we'd come home in a cab because there were no buses running to North Richmond they didn't start a bus until World War
